texts.orig: On the Coverage Required for Diploid Genome Assembly
texts.text: On the Coverage Required for Diploid Genome Assembly

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Daanish Mahajan, Chirag Jain, Navin Kashyap
texts.text: Daanish Mahajan, Chirag Jain, Navin Kashyap

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -The repeat content and heterozygosity rate of a target genome are important factors in determining the feasibility of achieving a complete telomere-to-telomere assembly. The mathematical relationship between the required coverage and read length for the purpose of unique reconstruction remains unexplored for diploid genomes. We investigate the informationtheoretic conditions that the given set of sequencing reads must satisfy to achieve the complete reconstruction of the true sequence of a diploid genome. We also analyze the standard greedy and de-Bruijn graph-based assembly algorithms. Our results show that the coverage and read length requirements of the assembly algorithms are considerably higher than the lower bound because both algorithms require the double repeats in the genome to be bridged. Finally, we derive the necessary conditions for the overlap graph-based assembly paradigm.
texts.text: Abstract -The repeat content and heterozygosity rate of a target genome are important factors in determining the feasibility of achieving a complete telomere-to-telomere assembly. The mathematical relationship between the required coverage and read length for the purpose of unique reconstruction remains unexplored for diploid genomes. We investigate the informationtheoretic conditions that the given set of sequencing reads must satisfy to achieve the complete reconstruction of the true sequence of a diploid genome. We also analyze the standard greedy and de-Bruijn graph-based assembly algorithms. Our results show that the coverage and read length requirements of the assembly algorithms are considerably higher than the lower bound because both algorithms require the double repeats in the genome to be bridged. Finally, we derive the necessary conditions for the overlap graph-based assembly paradigm.

texts.orig: The problem of reconstructing genomes, i.e., de novo genome assembly, is a fundamental problem in computational biology. DNA sequencing instruments cannot produce an individual's entire DNA sequence (genome) but produce many short strings (reads) sampled from random unknown genome locations. The de novo genome assembly problem seeks to reconstruct the original genome using the set of reads as input. The length and accuracy of reads depend on the choice of sequencing technology. Recent long-read sequencing instruments can produce reads of length above 10 kbp with a sequencing error rate below one percent [2], [3]. If a genome sequence of length 𝐺 is to be covered entirely with probability 1 -𝜖 using reads of length 𝐿 , then a lower bound on the count of reads is available from the Lander-Waterman statistics [4], [5], which is approximately 𝐺 𝐿 ln 𝐺 𝐿𝜖 . In the genome assembly problem, these reads are to be 'stitched together' without prior knowledge about the initial order in which they were present.

In the genome assembly problem, these reads are to be 'stitched together' without prior knowledge about the initial order in which they were present.

texts.orig: because of the size of the data and errors in the genome sequencing process [7]. Also, if repeats in a genome are longer than the length of reads, the parsimony-based approaches can affect the count of these repeats in the reconstructed sequence compared to the true sequence. Some practical efforts to solve the assembly problem include building a de-Bruijn graph and finding an Eulerian Superpath of the set of reads in the obtained graph [8]. The other approach includes building a read-overlap graph [9]. State-of-the-art assemblers, e.g., Hifiasm [10], Verkko [11], though built on top of the overlap graph and de Bruijn graph frameworks respectively, combine several other heuristics and multiple sequencing technologies to assemble reads into significantly longer substrings (contigs) of the genome. All this machinery is required because of the fundamental barrier of long repeats in a genome that cannot be bridged by a single read. This limitation can make the underlying genome's unique reconstruction impossible.

texts.orig: This is the basis of the information-optimal genome assembly problem, which asks for the minimum amount of information (read length and coverage depth) required under which the genome can be reconstructed successfully, i.e., with information less than this, no algorithm can guarantee reconstruction of the unknown genome sequence. Hence, these necessary conditions impose a fundamental limit on the performance of any assembly algorithm. Once we have the information-theoretic necessary conditions, the next task is to identify efficient algorithms and analyse the amount of information required (necessary and sufficient conditions) by them to reconstruct the ground truth genome. Motahari et al. [5] gave information-theoretic necessary conditions for genome assembly under i.i.d. and Markov model for DNA sequence. The follow-up work in [6], [12], and [13] provided the necessary conditions for arbitrary repeat statistics and gave sufficient conditions for the known assembly algorithms that are based on greedy, de Bruijn graph and overlap graph-based approaches.

texts.orig: The above works considered the haploid genome assembly problem. A haploid genome includes a single copy of each chromosome. There is limited information-theoretic research for diploid genomes, i.e., when the unknown genome comprises two near-identical copies of each chromosome inherited from both parents. For example, human genomes are diploid. The set of chromosomes inherited from a single parent is called a haplotype . The combined length of human chromosomes is about 3 billion nucleotides in each haplotype. The two haplotypes in a human genome are about 99 . 9% identical. These haplotypes differ due to mutations in the form of substitutions, insertions, deletions, etc. The loci at which

texts.orig: the two haplotypes differ are referred to as heterozygous loci. Identifying heterozygous loci imposes an additional challenge for any diploid genome assembler because a read does not contain information about the haplotype from which they originate. In case the read length is smaller than the maximum gap between two adjacent heterozygous loci, one obvious error an algorithm can make is to switch between paternal and maternal haplotypes during genome reconstruction. Separating the two haplotypes (also known as phasing ) is usually a post-processing step that requires long-range Hi-C data or sequencing data of the parents [14]. Si et al. [15] addressed a related problem of reference-based diploid genome assembly where a reference genome is used during assembly.

texts.orig: In this paper, we focus on de novo diploid genome assembly problem from an information-theoretic perspective. We derive (a) information-theoretic necessary conditions, (b) necessary and sufficient conditions for the greedy and de Bruijn graphbased assembly algorithms, and (c) necessary conditions for the overlap graph-based algorithm such that the assembled genome matches the ground truth up to switch errors. Figure 1(b) gives an illustration of an assembled diploid genome that matches the ground truth up to switch errors. An assembly with switch errors is also called as a partially phased assembly in bioinformatics literature [16].

An assembly with switch errors is also called as a partially phased assembly in bioinformatics literature [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We prove that all three algorithms (i.e., greedy, de Bruijn graph-based, and overlap graph-based algorithms) require bridging of double repeats to guarantee the reconstruction of the diploid genome up to switch errors. Our theoretical results imply that the information required by the assembly algorithms in terms of coverage depth and read length is significantly more for reconstructing diploid genomes than haploid genomes. We empirically evaluate the algorithms by using repeat statistics of a publicly available assembled sequence of human chromosome 19. This experiment sheds light on the information requirements of the assembly algorithms for an unassembled genome having similar statistics.

texts.orig: We assume that the unknown diploid genome comprises two circular strings. We enumerate the unknown pair of haplotype sequences as (H 0 , H 1 ) of length |H| each. Suppose the two haplotypes differ at exactly 𝑛 heterozygous loci (where 𝑛 is also unknown). We assume that the variation between the haplotypes is only due to substitution events. We enumerate the heterozygous loci by L 1 , L 2 , . . . , L 𝑛 in a clockwise manner starting from an arbitrary locus. Accordingly, H 0 [ 𝑖 ] = H 1 [ 𝑖 ] for all 𝑖 ∈ { 0 , . . . , |H| 1 } \ {L 1 , . . . , L 𝑛 } . Similarly, H 0 [ 𝑖 ] ≠ H 1 [ 𝑖 ] for all 𝑖 ∈ {L 1 , . . . , L 𝑛 } . Since the genome is circular, we define L 0 : = L 𝑛 and L 𝑛 + 1 : = L 1 . We make simplication assumptions that all input reads in a given set, say R , are error-free, single-stranded, and have fixed length 𝐿 . Each read is sampled uniformly and independently from one of the two haplotypes (Figure 2). The average number of times a nucleotide in the genome is sampled during a sequencing experiment is referred to as coverage depth . It can be calculated as | R | 𝐿 2 | H| .

The subclass of triple repeat with 𝑖 1 = 𝑖 2 = 𝑖 3 is called intra-triple repeat. Two pairs of strings ( 𝑠 𝑙 1 𝑖 1 , 𝑗 1 , 𝑠 𝑙 1 𝑖 2 , 𝑗 2 ) and ( 𝑠 𝑙 2 𝑖 3 , 𝑗 3 , 𝑠 𝑙 2 𝑖 4 , 𝑗 4 ) are referred to as an interleaved repeat if they are individually double repeats such that 𝑗 1 < 𝑗 3 < 𝑗 2 < 𝑗 4 and 𝑠 𝑙 1 𝑖 1 , 𝑗 1 ≠ 𝑠 𝑙 2 𝑖 3 , 𝑗 3 . texts.orig: A substring 𝑠 𝑙 𝑖, 𝑗 ( 𝑙 ≤ 𝐿 ) is said to be covered by a read 𝑟 if ∃ 𝑘 ∈ [ 𝑗 + 𝑙 -𝐿, 𝑗 ] such that 𝑠 𝐿 𝑖,𝑘 = 𝑟 . For example, in Figure 3, substring 𝑟 ′ 2 is covered by read 𝑥 𝑖 + 1 3 . A set of reads is said to cover the diploid genome if, for both haplotypes individually, every pair of adjacent nucleotides is covered by at least one read. A substring in the genome is said to be bridged by a read if it covers at least one base just before and one just after that substring. For example, in Figure 3, substring 𝑟 ′′ is bridged by read 𝑥 𝑖 1 . A double repeat or a triple repeat is said to be bridged if at least one copy of the repeat is bridged. An interleaved repeat is said to be bridged if at least one of the double repeat is bridged.
texts.text: A substring 𝑠 𝑙 𝑖, 𝑗 ( 𝑙 ≤ 𝐿 ) is said to be covered by a read 𝑟 if ∃ 𝑘 ∈ [ 𝑗 + 𝑙 -𝐿, 𝑗 ] such that 𝑠 𝐿 𝑖,𝑘 = 𝑟 . For example, in Figure 3, substring 𝑟 ′ 2 is covered by read 𝑥 𝑖 + 1 3 . texts.orig: 1) Algorithm: The greedy algorithm (Algorithm 1) works by merging pairs of reads in the decreasing order of the length of pairwise overlaps among them. Finally, the leftover sequences are processed to obtain two haplotype sequences.

texts.orig: Proof. Let R ′ be the maximal subset of R such that the reads belonging to R ′ do not cover a heterozygous locus. Condition G3 ensures that when the greedy algorithm is run with R\R ′ as input, we will get, at most, two directed open chains of reads at the end of the while loop. The output size will be one if an odd number of switch errors are made in the underlying haplotypes; otherwise, the output size will be two (refer to Figure 7 for an example). What is left to be proved is that the reads belonging to R ′ would only result in the subdivision 1 and extension of the output chains for the input R\R ′ and hence will not result in the formation of any additional chains.

texts.orig: Suppose the reads in R and R ′ are sorted in clockwise order based on their starting positions (hence based on their ending positions as well). Consider the minimum index 𝑎 , and then, the maximum index 𝑏 ( 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ [|R ′ |] ) such that OVERLAP (R ′ [ 𝑖 ] , R ′ [ 𝑖 + 1 ]) > 0 , ∀ 𝑖 ∈ [ 𝑎, 𝑏 ) . Next, consider the read 𝑢 just before read R ′ [ 𝑎 ] in R and the read 𝑣 just after read R ′ [ 𝑏 ] in R . The only way the greedy algorithm skips reads R ′ [ 𝑎 ] , . . . , R ′ [ 𝑏 ] is if it merges read 𝑢 with read 𝑣 . But since OVERLAP ( 𝑢, R ′ [ 𝑎 ]) > OVERLAP ( 𝑢, 𝑣 ) and OVERLAP (R ′ [ 𝑏 ] , 𝑣 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑢, 𝑣 ) , the greedy algorithm will merge the reads in the order 𝑢 → R ′ [ 𝑎 ] → . . . → R ′ [ 𝑏 ] → 𝑣 (note that OVERLAP ( 𝑢, R ′ [ 𝑎 ]) > 0 by virtue of Condition I3). We can extend this argument for indices greater than 𝑏 and keep doing the same till R ′ is exhausted. This proves the claim. □

texts.orig: Proof. Suppose read 𝑖 1 does not cover a heterozygous locus. Let 𝑘 be the minimum index such that read 𝑖 𝑘 covers a heterozygous locus L 𝑗 + 1 . Note that 𝑖 1 must belong to the region between the loci pair (L 𝑗 , L 𝑗 + 1 ) . Let us analyze the reads 𝑥 𝑗 1 , 𝑥 𝑗 2 , 𝑥 𝑗 3 , 𝑥 𝑗 4 . By definition, it is clear that 𝑖 𝑘 is either 𝑥 𝑗 3 or 𝑥 𝑗 4 . WLOG, let it be 𝑥 𝑗 3 . Now, since the output is a single string formed by the iterative merging of all the reads (including 𝑥 𝑗 3 and 𝑥 𝑗 4 ), there exists a contiguous sequence of reads 𝑖 𝑎 , 𝑖 𝑎 + 1 , . . . , 𝑖 𝑏 , in the output string where 𝑘 < 𝑎 ≤ 𝑏 < |R| and 𝑖 𝑎 = 𝑥 𝑗 1 or 𝑥 𝑗 2 and 𝑖 𝑏 = 𝑥 𝑗 4 . Since the endpoint of 𝑖 1 is after the endpoint of 𝑖 𝑎 and the start point of 𝑖 1 is before the start point of 𝑖 𝑏 , as a result of Condition I3 there exists some index 𝑐 ∈ [ 𝑎, 𝑏 ) , such that OVERLAP ( 𝑖 𝑐 , 𝑖 1 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑖 𝑐 , 𝑖 𝑐 + 1 ) , which is a contradiction (since 𝑖 1 will not then be the first read in the output). See Figure 8 for an illustration. A similar argument can be used to prove the claim for read 𝑖 | R | . □

texts.orig: Proof. We consider two cases. Case (1) 𝑖 | R | and 𝑖 1 do not cover a common heterozygous locus. From Claim 5, we know that 𝑖 | R | ∈ { 𝑥 𝑝 1 , 𝑥 𝑝 2 } and 𝑖 1 ∈ { 𝑥 𝑞 -1 3 , 𝑥 𝑞 -1 4 } . We further argue that 𝑝 = 𝑞 -1 , else the region from the end of 𝑖 | R | to the start of 𝑖 1 is ignored in the reconstruction of one of the haplotypes. Condition G3a ensures that 𝑖 | R | and 𝑖 1 overlap. Case (2) 𝑖 | R | and 𝑖 1 cover a common heterozygous locus. 𝑖 | R | and 𝑖 1 must cover the same haplotype, else again the region from the end of 𝑖 | R | to the start of 𝑖 1 is ignored in a haplotype. □

texts.orig: 1) Algorithm: First, an edge-centric de Bruijn graph [17] is built using reads R and is denoted by DB 𝑘 𝑒𝑐 (R) . This is a graph in which the nodes are the set of all 𝑘 -length substrings ( 𝑘 -mers) occurring in R , 𝑘 < 𝐿 , and there is an edge from node 𝑢 to node 𝑣 iff OVERLAP ( 𝑢, 𝑣 ) = 𝑘 -1 and UNION ( 𝑢, 𝑣 ) is a substring of some read in R . Next, all maximal unitigs 2 are compacted into a single vertex. Since the sequence represented by some nodes is now longer than 𝑘 , this is no longer a 𝑘 -mer graph and is denoted by the condensed sequence graph 𝐺 𝑐 .

texts.orig: If all pairs of adjacent heterozygous loci are separated by at most 𝑘 -1 nucleotides and 𝑘 is greater than the maximum length of an inter-double repeat, then 𝐺 𝑐 will contain two connected components representing individual haplotypes. In this case, the subsequent conditions for the successful reconstruction of the two haplotypes will be analogous to the ones for haploid genomes [12]. Therefore, our further analysis assumes that 𝐺 𝑐 is composed of a single connected component. We aim to find the Eulerian circuit of 𝐺 𝑐 such that the string spelled by the circuit represents the concatenation of two haplotypes up to switch errors. The string spelled by the walk is computed by beginning from the label of a starting vertex and appending the label of the other vertices (excluding the ending vertex in the walk, which is the same as the starting vertex) in the order they are visited after removing the 𝑘 -1 length prefix of those labels. Finally, the 𝑘 -1 length suffix of the string obtained is trimmed since that is covered in the label of the starting vertex (Algorithm 2).

texts.orig: 1) Algorithm: A read overlap graph is constructed by adding directed edges between a pair of nodes (reads) if they share a suffix-prefix overlap, and the weight of the edge is considered to be the length of the maximum overlap. In the overlap graph, if 𝑥 overlaps 𝑦 , 𝑦 overlaps 𝑧 , 𝑥 overlaps 𝑧 , and UNION ( UNION ( 𝑥, 𝑦 ) , 𝑧 ) = UNION ( 𝑥, 𝑧 ) , then the edge from 𝑥 to 𝑧 is said to be transitively inferable . Such edges are redundant and are therefore excluded from the graph [9]. Overlap graph 𝐺 ′ obtained after removing all the transitively inferable edges is fed as an input to the assembly algorithm. Appealing to parsimony, one way to formulate the assembly problem is to find the shortest node-covering closed walk in graph 𝐺 ′ . Finally, the string spelled by this walk is processed similarly to Algorithm 2 to report the reconstructed haplotypes.

texts.orig: We apply our theoretical analysis to compute the coverage depth and read length requirements for the assembly of human chromosome 19 . For this experiment, we used an already assembled sequence of the maternal copy of chromosome 19 in the HG002 genome. This sequence was assembled by the Telomere-to-telomere consortium [18]. We simulated heterozygous substitution events to obtain the paternal sequence. We opted to simulate these variants rather than using real variation data because the set of high-quality variant calls in HG002 genome released by the Genome in a Bottle consortium are only available in a fraction of the genome (high confidence intervals).

texts.orig: Comparing Figure 13(a) with Figure 13(b), we observe that the orange curve associated with the de Bruijn graph-based algorithm shifts right as we increase ploidy. The conditions of the de Bruijn graph-based algorithm for haploid genome reconstruction are with respect to interleaved and triple repeats [12], whereas the conditions that we derived involve double repeats of the target diploid genome. By definition, bridging of all double repeats ensures that all interleaved and triple repeats are bridged, but the opposite does not hold.

texts.orig: In this paper, we present the first rigorous informationtheoretic analysis for the problem of diploid genome assembly. Diploid genome assembly is a fundamental problem in computational biology; for example, it is used to study an individual's genome accurately without reference bias and to build human pangenome references. Compared to haploid genomes, the information-theoretic analysis for diploid genomes is more challenging due to the presence of heterozygous variation and the increase in the number of classes and arrangements of repeats.

Diploid genome assembly is a fundamental problem in computational biology; for example, it is used to study an individual's genome accurately without reference bias and to build human pangenome references. texts.orig: The proposed information-theoretic conditions in Section III do not deviate significantly from the already known conditions for haploid genome reconstruction. We do not expect our Condition I2 to influence the read length and coverage requirements on real genome sequences. This is also evident from our experiments using human chromosome 19.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While analysing the standard assembly algorithms in Section IV, we showed that their information requirement for the correct reconstruction of a diploid genome is significantly higher compared to the known conditions for a haploid genome. The performance of these algorithms is limited by the maximum length of the double repeat. Meeting these read length and coverage requirements with error-free reads is currently infeasible in practice, which justifies the need for heuristics for generating complete telomere-to-telomere genome assemblies.

texts.orig: 2) Greedy algorithm: We first analyze the error probability due to Condition G3a in Section IV-A2. Consider a pair of adjacent loci (L 𝑖 , L 𝑖 + 1 ) . We fix the right endpoint of read 𝑥 𝑖 1 (let it be 𝑗 ). The probability of sampling such a read equals 1 𝐺 . Next, we find the probability that read 𝑥 𝑖 2 has its right endpoint ahead of read 𝑥 𝑖 1 that equals 𝑔 𝑖 -𝑗 + 1 𝐺 . For condition G3a to be satisfied, the homozygous region [L 𝑖 + 𝑗 + 1 , L 𝑖 + 𝑔 𝑗 ] must be bridged by at least one read on both haplotypes. The same can be calculated by first fixing the haplotype where this region is not bridged and then multiplying it with 𝑝 unbridged 𝑔 𝑖 -𝑗 . A factor of 2 is multiplied to account for the case when the read 𝑥 𝑖 1 has its right endpoint ahead of read 𝑥 𝑖 2 . Also, an additional term is added for the gaps where a single read can't cover the adjacent loci, i.e., the probability that the region [L 𝑖 + 1 , L 𝑖 + 𝑔 𝑖 -𝐿 + 1 ] is not bridged by at least one read on at least one of the haplotypes. Using union bound, the total probability of error, 𝑃 2 err , will be bounded by the sum of these probabilities. Thus, 𝑃 2 err is upper bounded by:

We fix the right endpoint of read 𝑥 𝑖 1 (let it be 𝑗 ). The probability of sampling such a read equals 1 𝐺 . Next, we find the probability that read 𝑥 𝑖 2 has its right endpoint ahead of read 𝑥 𝑖 1 that equals 𝑔 𝑖 -𝑗 + 1 𝐺 . For condition G3a to be satisfied, the homozygous region [L 𝑖 + 𝑗 + 1 , L 𝑖 + 𝑔 𝑗 ] must be bridged by at least one read on both haplotypes. The same can be calculated by first fixing the haplotype where this region is not bridged and then multiplying it with 𝑝 unbridged 𝑔 𝑖 -𝑗 . texts.orig: Fig. 14: Figure shows different possible arrangements of a double repeat; for all cases, the double repeat is not wellbridged. (a) Both copies of the double repeat ( 𝑟 1 , 𝑟 2 ) lie between the same pair of heterozygous loci and overlap. Note that the reads 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 1 2 , 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 1 4 satisfy condition G3a because of the presence of repeats. Now, greedy algorithm will merge 𝑥 1 1 → 𝑥 6 → 𝑥 5 → 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 1 2 → 𝑥 1 4 . Although a gap exists between reads 𝑥 1 2 and 𝑥 1 4 , it is overlooked because of repeats. (b) The double repeat copies belong to the homozygous region between different pairs of heterozygous loci. Here OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 2 4 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 1 4 ) and OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 2 4 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 4 , 𝑥 2 4 ) . Greedy algorithm will merge 𝑥 1 2 → 𝑥 1 3 → 𝑥 2 1 → 𝑥 2 3 , 𝑥 1 1 → 𝑥 2 4 , thereby leaving read 𝑥 1 4 unmerged. (c) Exactly one repeat copy of the double repeat covers a heterozygous locus and the two copies do not overlap. Here, OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 2 4 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 2 1 ) . Greedy algorithm will merge 𝑥 1 1 → 𝑥 1 3 → 𝑥 2 4 , 𝑥 1 2 → 𝑥 1 4 , 𝑥 2 1 → 𝑥 2 3 . (d) Both the repeat copies cover a different heterozygous locus. Here, OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 2 4 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 2 1 ) and OVERLAP ( 𝑥 1 3 , 𝑥 2 4 ) > OVERLAP ( 𝑥 2 2 , 𝑥 2 4 ) . Greedy algorithm will merge 𝑥 1 1 → 𝑥 1 3 → 𝑥 2 4 , 𝑥 1 4 → 𝑥 2 2 , 𝑥 2 1 → 𝑥 2 3 . (e) The repeat copies cover the same heterozygous locus but belong to different haplotypes. Here, the reads 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 1 4 have the maximum suffix-prefix overlap. The overlap between reads 𝑥 1 1 and 𝑥 1 4 is greater because of the presence of repeats than when the repeat is absent. (f) The repeat copies cover the same heterozygous locus and belong to the same haplotype. Like case (e), overlap between reads 𝑥 1 1 , 𝑥 1 3 will be greater than when the repeat is absent. Note: Figure (a) can be extended for the case when the repeat copies do not overlap. Similarly, Figure (c) can be extended for the cases when the repeat copies overlap, or the copies do not overlap and have at least one heterozygous locus between the close ends of the copies that is not covered by any of the copies.
